column2: Assessment of Sign Language-Based versus Touch-Based Input for Deaf Users Interacting with Intelligent Personal Assistants
column5: Participants' ASL sign language commands were literally interpreted and converted into voice commands relayed to Alexa (simulating language translation), while managing linguistic interaction logic—including wake-word handling (recognizing fingerspelled "Alexa" or name gestures) and interpreting commands when the wake word was omitted. The operator remotely controlled the laptop at the "Dorothy" station via VNC Viewer, observed participants' signing through a FaceTime session, recorded video using the Photo Booth application, and simultaneously transmitted voice commands to Alexa via microphone to trigger environmental devices (e.g., lights, Fire TV). The simulated system also handled non-responsive scenarios (e.g., refraining from repeating commands and waiting for participant decisions) and addressed incomprehensible signs by triggering "Alexa, write" to prompt participants to retry.
column6: Communication Control, Embodied & Environmental Action Control, System Logic Simulation Control
column7:
• “Using this methodology, we can simulate machine sign language recognition technologies to better gauge their usability.” 
  • “In particular, the “Dorothy” station provided the smart home environment to participants,… It was controlled remotely by the researcher at the “Wizard” station.” 
  • “For ASL to Alexa, using the Wizard-of-Oz method, …communicating the sign language commands to Alexa.” 
  • “To facilitate communication between deaf participants and Alexa in the ASL…the Wizard was required to use literal interpretation for each session.”
column8: Alignment with Prior Work, Technical Limitation
column9:
- “Many of the signs used show potential for interpreter error, and its correct interpretation depends on the Wizard’s understanding of the context. Not all fingerspelling is crisp and clear, and participants sometimes produced fingerspelled words with partially produced or omitted letters.””
- “The sign language to spoken English chain of transmission for commands in the Wizard-of-Oz setup also is a potential limiting factor. Due to the interpreting, there was additional lag time between the ASL input of the participant’s command and Alexa’s response and corresponding processing, compared to voice-only interaction. The need for audio from the Wizard to issue commands also resulted in problems and frustration that would not exist in a hypothetical ASL-based IPA.”
+ “To further minimize possible interpretation biases, the Wizard was only informed of the condition order, but not of the specifics of each task list. This approach is in marked contrast to what interpreter training typically practices, as most other scenarios require interpreters to use functionally/dynamically equivalent interpretation. In the latter situation, interpreters voice the concepts that are implied by what they see [42]. This created tension for the Wizard and required them to be continually monitored by the deaf research team to ensure they did not inadvertently step out of their role.”

column2: How People Prompt Generative AI to Create Interactive VR Scenes
column5:
1. Altering System State: "Participants experienced the system as if it was a fully-functional system; in fact, it was a Wizard of Oz protocol, where the state of the system was changed by a second experimenter." This indicates that one of the wizard’s primary responsibilities was to modify the system state, enabling participants to perceive the system as fully functional.
2. Operating a Custom Application to Control the VR World: "In both cases, the second experimenter changed the state of the VR world via a laptop PC. The laptop used a custom node.js application which controlled the state of the VR world. This state change had no latency (beyond the experimenter pressing the button). The application presented a GUI control panel that allowed the experimenter to change to any hard coded scene and step in the entire protocol at the press of a button." This demonstrates that the wizard manipulated the custom node.js application through the GUI control panel to dynamically update the VR environment and advance the experimental protocol.

Controlling the robot-customer interaction process: When a customer approaches the robot, the human operator (wizard) initiates a welcome message and asks whether assistance is needed; based on the customer’s response, the operator attempts to resolve issues while modulating the conversation’s duration, depth, and informational content.
2. Operating the robot’s movements and verbal output: Through a custom-developed user interface, the operator freely controls the robot’s motion, triggers animations, and executes German text-to-speech (TTS) outputs.
3. Perceiving the environment and customer queries: Using the robot’s camera and microphone, the operator monitors the surroundings and receives customer inquiries in real time.
4. Sustaining conversation flow: To maintain natural dialogue progression, the operator is equipped with a shopping mall layout map and consults Google Search as needed to retrieve detailed product information.

column5:

Specifically, the wizard corrects errors generated by the server during the alignment and categorization of revised content before participants begin the second round of revisions.
2. Categorizing revised content: The ArgRewrite server automatically extracts and categorizes revised content. Within the experimental protocol, the wizard verifies and refines these categorization results to ensure accuracy. In conditions B, C, and D, the wizard confirms whether the student agrees with the category of the revised sentence presented by the tool; students are required to provide this confirmation both before and after completing their final revision.
